questions.question: What is the primary structural advantage transformers have over RNNs that makes them much faster to train on GPUs?
options: They process all positions of a sequence in parallel rather than one step at a time
They use far fewer parameters than a comparable RNN
They avoid floating-point matrix multiplication entirely
They process sequences in O(n) memory instead of O(n²)
questions.explanation: An RNN must compute step t before t+1 (n non-parallel steps), whereas a transformer computes all positions in one matrix multiply, giving far better GPU utilization. The memory distractor is backwards: attention costs O(n²) memory, more than the RNN's O(n).

questions.question: In attention, which vector represents the actual **content** a token contributes to the blend if it is attended to?
options: The value (V)
The query (Q)
The key (K)
The positional encoding
questions.explanation: The value is the content a token contributes when attended to. The query is what a token is looking for, and the key is what a token advertises about itself for matching.

questions.question: In `softmax(Q Kᵀ / √d_k) · V`, the softmax is applied row-wise. What does each row of the resulting weight matrix represent?
options: One query's attention distribution over all keys, summing to 1
One key's similarity to all queries, summing to d_k
The mean of the value vectors across all positions
The variance of the pre-softmax scores for each head
questions.explanation: Softmax is applied row-wise so each row turns one query's scores into a probability distribution over all keys that sums to 1. The other options misdescribe what a row holds.

questions.question: Without positional encoding, a transformer's self-attention is permutation-equivariant, so it cannot distinguish "dog bites man" from "man bites dog."
questions.explanation: Attention is a weighted average over an unordered set; permuting the input tokens permutes the outputs identically. Positional encoding adds an order-revealing signal to break this symmetry.

questions.question: Which transformer family uses bidirectional self-attention and is the natural choice for classification, embeddings, and retrieval?
options: Encoder-only (BERT-style)
Decoder-only (GPT-style)
Encoder-decoder (T5-style)
A recurrent encoder with attention
questions.explanation: Encoder-only models stack bidirectional self-attention blocks for understanding tasks like classification and retrieval. Decoder-only is causal/generative, and encoder-decoder targets sequence-to-sequence mapping.

questions.question: Why are the raw dot-product scores divided by `√d_k` before the softmax?
options: Dot-product variance grows with d_k, and large scores saturate softmax into a near-one-hot region where its gradient is almost zero
It converts the scores into integers so the softmax is numerically exact
It guarantees the attention weights become exactly uniform across positions
It compensates for the number of attention heads h so each head sums to 1
questions.explanation: Dot products of d_k-dimensional unit-variance vectors have variance ≈ d_k, so they grow with dimension; large pre-softmax scores push softmax toward one-hot where the gradient vanishes and learning stalls. Dividing by √d_k rescales variance back toward 1.

questions.question: In a translation model's cross-attention layer, where do the Query, Key, and Value come from?
options: Q from the decoder sequence; K and V from the encoder sequence
Q, K, and V all from the encoder sequence
Q and K from the decoder; V from the encoder
Q from the encoder; K and V from the decoder
questions.explanation: Cross-attention takes Q from the decoder (the output-so-far) and K, V from the encoder's representation of the source, letting the decoder consult the source text. The math is identical to self-attention; only the input wiring differs.

questions.question: A model has `d_model = 512` and `h = 8` heads. What is the per-head dimension, and how does the total attention cost compare to a single full-width head?
options: d_k = 64 per head; the 8 heads together cost about the same as one full-width head
d_k = 512 per head; the 8 heads cost roughly 8× a single full-width head
d_k = 64 per head; the 8 heads cost roughly 8× a single full-width head
d_k = 4096 per head; cost is dominated by the final Wo projection
questions.explanation: Per-head dimension is d_model/h = 512/8 = 64. Because each head is 1/h as wide, h heads together cost about the same as one full-width head, buying representational diversity nearly for free.

questions.question: Why do transformers use layer normalization rather than batch normalization?
options: Layer norm normalizes per token across features and needs no batch statistics, which are awkward for variable-length sequences
Batch norm cannot be implemented on GPUs, but layer norm can
Layer norm removes the need for residual connections in deep stacks
Batch norm only works for image data and produces NaNs on any text input
questions.explanation: Layer norm is computed per token across its features and needs no batch statistics, which are unstable for variable-length, variable-padding sequences and small inference batches. The other options are false claims about both techniques.

questions.question: How is causal masking implemented in a decoder's self-attention?
options: Add −∞ to the future (disallowed) entries of the score matrix before softmax, so those weights become exactly 0
Multiply the value vectors of future tokens by 0 after the softmax has been applied
Drop future tokens from the input sequence entirely before the layer runs
Set the future entries of the score matrix to 0 before applying the softmax
questions.explanation: Adding −∞ before softmax forces e^(−∞)=0, so future weights become exactly 0; this is the lower-triangular mask. Setting scores to 0 before softmax would instead give those positions weight e^0=1 (nonzero), which is wrong, and zeroing values after softmax still lets the normalization mass leak.

questions.question: Roughly how does the attention compute and memory cost change when you double the sequence length n?
options: It roughly quadruples, because the score matrix is n × n (O(n²))
It roughly doubles, because attention is linear in n
It stays the same, because heads are run in parallel
It grows by about log n, like a convolutional layer
questions.explanation: Self-attention is O(n²·d) time and O(n²) memory because the score matrix is n × n, so doubling n roughly quadruples attention cost. This quadratic term is the main obstacle to very long context windows.

questions.question: Compared to a recurrent layer, which statements about a self-attention layer are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: It has O(1) sequential operations (fully parallel) versus the RNN's O(n)
It has O(1) maximum path length between any two tokens versus the RNN's O(n)
Its per-layer time complexity is O(n²·d) versus the RNN's O(n·d²)
It uses less memory in sequence length than the RNN
It cannot model long-range dependencies as directly as the RNN
questions.explanation: Self-attention is O(n²·d) with O(1) sequential steps and O(1) max path length; the RNN is O(n·d²) with O(n) sequential steps and O(n) path length. It uses MORE memory (O(n²)) and models long-range dependencies MORE directly, so those two options are false.

questions.question: Given query `q = [2, 0]` and keys `k1 = [1, 0]`, `k2 = [0, 1]` with `d_k = 2`, what are the attention weights (to 3 decimals)?
questions.code:
d_k = 2,  sqrt(d_k) ≈ 1.414
scores: q·k1, q·k2  ->  scale by 1/1.414  ->  softmax
questions.explanation: q·k1 = 2, q·k2 = 0; scaled by 1/1.414 gives 1.414 and 0. Softmax: e^1.414 ≈ 4.113, e^0 = 1, sum 5.113, so weights ≈ [0.804, 0.196]. [0.5, 0.5] would require equal scores; [0.881, 0.119] omits the √d_k scaling.

questions.question: Which statements about one transformer block are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Each sublayer output takes the form x + Sublayer(x) via a residual connection
The feed-forward sublayer is applied to each position independently
It contains exactly two sublayers: multi-head attention and a position-wise FFN
The residual connection replaces the need for an attention sublayer
The FFN mixes information across positions, which is the 'communication' step
questions.explanation: A block has two sublayers (multi-head attention then a position-wise FFN), each wrapped as x + Sublayer(x) with a layer norm, and the FFN runs per position. Attention is the cross-position 'communication' step (not the FFN), and residuals supplement rather than replace the attention sublayer.

questions.question: A decoder is trained without a causal mask. Training loss looks excellent, but generation quality is terrible. What is the precise cause?
options: Each position attended to future tokens during training, so the model learned to predict the next token partly by reading information unavailable at generation time
The missing mask made gradients explode, so the weights never converged at all
Softmax could no longer normalize because some scores were −∞
The model became permutation-equivariant and lost all positional information
questions.explanation: Without the mask, each position can read its own future, making next-token prediction trivially easy during training (low loss) but impossible to reproduce at inference where the future doesn't exist yet. The other options describe unrelated failure modes.

questions.question: In the original sinusoidal scheme, lower dimension indices use higher-frequency sinusoids (changing quickly with position) while higher dimension indices use lower-frequency ones (changing slowly), and these encodings are added to the token embeddings.
questions.explanation: Sinusoidal PE uses sin/cos of geometrically varying frequency: freq(0)=1 (fast, low dims) down to far smaller frequencies (slow, high dims), giving each position a unique fingerprint that is added to the embedding. The low dims (0,1) change quickly while the high dims (2,3) change slowly.

questions.question: Which of the following are techniques to mitigate the O(n²) attention bottleneck for long contexts? (Select all that apply.)
options: Sparse or local attention (each token attends to a window or a few global tokens)
Memory-efficient exact attention such as FlashAttention, which avoids materializing the full n × n matrix
Retrieval so the model reads only relevant chunks
Replacing self-attention layers with recurrent layers to regain O(1) memory
Increasing d_model so the n² term becomes negligible
questions.explanation: Sparse/local attention, linear approximations, FlashAttention, and retrieval are standard mitigations. Recurrent layers reintroduce O(n) sequential steps (and aren't O(1) memory), and raising d_model increases cost rather than removing the n² term.

questions.question: Which statement about normalization placement in the transformer block is correct?
options: The original paper used post-norm (normalize after the residual add); most modern large models use pre-norm because it trains more stably at depth
The original paper used pre-norm; modern models switched to post-norm for better extrapolation
Both pre-norm and post-norm were introduced in the original paper as equal options
Pre-norm eliminates the residual connection, whereas post-norm keeps it
questions.explanation: The original transformer normalized after the residual add (post-norm); most modern large models normalize before the sublayer (pre-norm) for more stable deep training. Either way, the residual + norm pairing is retained, so the last option is wrong.
